content:
I'm trying to build some minimal ieee80211 abstractions. I'm using newtype pattern with #[repr(transparent)] and then wrapping the c type in Unsafecell to work with c structs.
But ieee80211 related structs are huge and creating functions to manipulate all the struct fields is not easy. Is there a safe way to implement deref trait on these types so I can just work with struct fields directly in rust? I couldn't find any examples. Or is there a better pattern I can use?

I am not familiar with that API, but if possible, I think it is better to access the fields with the C API. That way you know locking is correct. If you access from Rust you need to make sure that you are serializing access correctly. I don't think you can do that without some code. You also need to consider that C side of kernel can change over time, if you use C API to access, you are better protected from that.

Generally, I'm using the C API where I can. But mac80211 drivers just directly manipulate structs fields in this api. Specially when setting things up, for an example, here, this driver allocates a ieee80211_hw struct and then sets wiphy->bands field directly.

I'm creating two #[repr(transparent)] rust structs for both wiphy and ieee80211_hw and and then using associated functions to set these fields now. But if I can implement deref, I don't have to create a function per field.
My suggestion is to just create the functions. :woman_shrugging:
If there really are so many, use a macro_rules! to do it.
So I guess that means there is no way to implement deref here?

If you want to prove direct access to the fields, then you must ensure that all of the fields are fully initialized and safe to access for both reading and writing. The fact that you need an UnsafeCell or Opaque means that this is not the case for you.
(Alternatively, if all of the fields are safe to access, then you can drop the UnsafeCell.)

Okay, I think at least ieee80211_hw struct should be fully initialized. so I can do something like this?

#[repr(transparent)]
struct Ieee80211Hw(*mut bindings::ieee80211_hw)
oh probably struct Ieee80211Hw(bindings::ieee80211_hw)

If all fields are initialized and safe to access, then that's fine, and it's also fine to implement Deref directly to ieee80211_hw.

However, you'll need a lifetime on the struct if you make it a raw pointer.
Okay, that's great help already, I'll try this and see how it goes. thank you very much :)
You still need to make sure that you uphold the safety invariants of UnsafeCell https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/cell/struct.UnsafeCell.html
Not if there's no UnsafeCell?
Right, not if there is no UnsafeCell. But I assume this data structure is shared with C code that will mutate the data? In that case it is not sound to omit UnsafeCell, right?

Madushan Nishantha said:

Okay, I think at least ieee80211_hw struct should be fully initialized. so I can do something like this?

#[repr(transparent)]
struct Ieee80211Hw(*mut bindings::ieee80211_hw)

If you mutate through a pointer, you are fine, no need for UnsafeCell, as long as you do not create references to the pointee that you hand off to you Rust code. I got the impression the data was owned.

Andreas Hindborg said:

But I assume this data structure is shared with C code that will mutate the data? In that case it is not sound to omit UnsafeCell, right?

The UnsafeCell is only needed if someone (C or Rust doesn't matter) modifies the value at the same time as when Rust code holds an immutable reference to it.
You still need to obey reference aliasing rules when you dereference the pointer.

As a general rule, if ptr::read is ok, then creating an immutable reference, reading, and then not using the immutable reference again is also sound. The same applies to mutable references and ptr::write.
As far as I know dereferencing a raw pointer expands to casting to a reference and mutating through that reference

Andreas Hindborg said:

As far as I know dereferencing a raw pointer expands to casting to a reference and mutating through that reference

Mutating or reading. Is that a correct assumtion @Alice Ryhl ?
References are always created by field accesses, though just doing *ptr = 5; doesn't actually make a reference for Copy types.

The bindgen struct looks like this

#[repr(C)]
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]
pub struct ieee80211_hw {
    pub conf: ieee80211_conf,
    pub wiphy: *mut wiphy,
    pub rate_control_algorithm: *const core::ffi::c_char,
    pub priv_: *mut core::ffi::c_void,
    pub flags: [core::ffi::c_ulong; 1usize],
    pub extra_tx_headroom: core::ffi::c_uint,
    pub extra_beacon_tailroom: core::ffi::c_uint,
    pub vif_data_size: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub sta_data_size: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub chanctx_data_size: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub txq_data_size: core::ffi::c_int,
    pub queues: u16_,
    pub max_listen_interval: u16_,
    pub max_signal: s8,
    pub max_rates: u8_,
    pub max_report_rates: u8_,
    pub max_rate_tries: u8_,
    pub max_rx_aggregation_subframes: u16_,
    pub max_tx_aggregation_subframes: u16_,
    pub max_tx_fragments: u8_,
    pub offchannel_tx_hw_queue: u8_,
    pub radiotap_mcs_details: u8_,
    pub radiotap_vht_details: u16_,
    pub radiotap_timestamp: ieee80211_hw__bindgen_ty_1,
    pub netdev_features: netdev_features_t,
    pub uapsd_queues: u8_,
    pub uapsd_max_sp_len: u8_,
    pub max_nan_de_entries: u8_,
    pub tx_sk_pacing_shift: u8_,
    pub weight_multiplier: u8_,
    pub max_mtu: u32_,
    pub tx_power_levels: *const s8,
    pub max_txpwr_levels_idx: u8_,
}

ieee80211_alloc_hw c function allocate the c struct. and then initialize it to a sane state. It also allocates the wiphy field but I'm not sure it initialize all the fields. Is implementing deref for 

#[repr(transparent)]
struct Ieee80211Hw(bindings::ieee80211_hw)

safe in this case?
If you give safe code access to that, then you must assume that all of the raw pointers have been broken and are now dangling pointers.
Okay, thank you.
